seo.title: Ayutthaya: Complete Travel Guide 2026 — Top Things To Do
seo.metaDescription: Plan your visit to Ayutthaya, Thailand's ancient capital and UNESCO World Heritage Site. Temple ruins, entrance fees, day trip tips from Bangkok and local food guide. Updated 2026.

overview: Ayutthaya (Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya) was the capital of the Kingdom of Siam for 417 years, from 1350 to 1767, when it was one of the largest and most cosmopolitan cities in the world. Today the Ayutthaya Historical Park — a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1991 — preserves the ruins of hundreds of temples, monasteries and palaces across an island formed by the confluence of three rivers: the Chao Phraya, Lopburi and Pa Sak. Located just 80 km north of Bangkok, Ayutthaya is one of Thailand's most popular day trips but rewards overnight visitors with quieter mornings, illuminated temples at dusk and the chance to explore beyond the main circuit.
highlights: UNESCO World Heritage Site — the ruins of the former Siamese capital, destroyed by the Burmese in 1767
Wat Mahathat — the iconic Buddha head entwined in banyan tree roots, one of Thailand's most photographed images
Wat Phra Si Sanphet — three majestic chedis that served as the royal temple within the old palace grounds
Wat Chaiwatthanaram — a riverside Khmer-style temple modelled after Angkor Wat, stunning at sunset
Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon — towering chedi surrounded by rows of saffron-draped Buddha statues
Bang Pa-In Royal Palace — an eclectic mix of Thai, Chinese and European architectural styles 20 km south
Roti sai mai — Ayutthaya's signature street food: candy floss wrapped in thin roti pancakes
Bicycle exploring — the flat, compact island is perfect for cycling between temple ruins at your own pace
Ayutthaya Floating Market — a tourist-oriented but photogenic floating market with boat rides and local food
Evening boat cruise — see illuminated temples reflected in the river as the sun sets

bestTimeToVisit.peak: November to February — cool and dry with temperatures 22–32°C. The best weather for cycling between temples and spending full days outdoors. This is high season; hotels fill up on weekends.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: March to May — hot season (35–40°C). Still manageable for early-morning temple visits but midday heat is intense. Songkran in April is celebrated enthusiastically in Ayutthaya.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: June to October — monsoon season brings heavy afternoon rain and occasional flooding in low-lying areas. The upside: fewer tourists, lush green landscapes and atmospheric misty ruins. October floods can close some riverside temples.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: November to February is ideal for Ayutthaya. If visiting as a day trip from Bangkok, any time of year works — just start early to beat the heat and afternoon rain.

topAttractions.name: Wat Mahathat
topAttractions.description: The most iconic temple in Ayutthaya, Wat Mahathat is famous for the serene Buddha head entwined in the roots of a banyan tree — one of the most photographed images in Thailand. Founded in 1374 during the reign of King Borommaracha I, the temple once held a sacred relic of the Buddha. The main prang (tower) collapsed long ago, but the atmospheric ruins, headless Buddha statues and the tree-root Buddha head make this an unmissable stop.
topAttractions.location: Ayutthaya Historical Park, Tha Wasukri, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB (~1.40 USD) per person, or included in 220 THB (~6 USD) six-temple combo ticket
tips: Arrive before 09:00 for the best photos of the Buddha head without crowds
You must crouch below the Buddha head when photographing — standing above it is disrespectful
Open daily 08:00–18:00
Combine with Wat Ratchaburana directly opposite for a single visit
Dress code: cover shoulders and knees

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Si Sanphet
topAttractions.description: The most important temple within the Ayutthaya palace grounds, Wat Phra Si Sanphet served the same role as the Grand Palace's Wat Phra Kaew in Bangkok. Its three iconic bell-shaped chedis, built between 1448 and 1530, once contained the ashes of Ayutthayan kings. The temple originally housed a 16-metre standing Buddha covered in 340 kg of gold, which the Burmese melted down when they sacked the city in 1767.
topAttractions.location: Ayutthaya Historical Park, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB (~1.40 USD) per person, or included in 220 THB (~6 USD) combo ticket
tips: Best photographed in late afternoon light when the three chedis glow golden
Adjacent to the former Royal Palace site — allow time to explore both
Open daily 08:00–18:00
Less crowded than Wat Mahathat despite being equally impressive

topAttractions.name: Wat Chaiwatthanaram
topAttractions.description: Built in 1630 by King Prasat Thong in Khmer style, Wat Chaiwatthanaram is Ayutthaya's most photogenic temple — especially at sunset when the riverside ruins glow orange against the darkening sky. The central 35-metre prang is surrounded by eight chedi-shaped chapels in a layout inspired by Angkor Wat. After extensive restoration, it is now one of the best-preserved temples in the historical park.
topAttractions.location: West bank of the Chao Phraya River, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB (~1.40 USD) per person, or included in 220 THB (~6 USD) combo ticket
tips: Come at 17:00–18:00 for the famous sunset view — the west-facing riverside position is perfect
Located outside the island — take a tuk-tuk or cycle across the bridge (2 km from the centre)
One of the temples illuminated at night — visible from evening river cruises
Open daily 08:00–18:00

topAttractions.name: Wat Ratchaburana
topAttractions.description: Built in 1424 by King Borommaracha II on the cremation site of his two older brothers who killed each other fighting for the throne, Wat Ratchaburana features a well-preserved central prang that you can descend into via a narrow staircase. Inside the crypt, faded original frescoes — some of the oldest surviving paintings in Thailand — still adorn the walls. Gold artefacts found here are displayed at the Chao Sam Phraya National Museum.
topAttractions.location: Ayutthaya Historical Park, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya
topAttractions.entranceFee: 50 THB (~1.40 USD) per person, or included in 220 THB (~6 USD) combo ticket
tips: The descent into the crypt is steep and narrow — not suitable for claustrophobic visitors
Directly opposite Wat Mahathat — visit both together
The original gold treasures found here are at the Chao Sam Phraya National Museum nearby
Open daily 08:00–18:00

topAttractions.name: Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon
topAttractions.description: Originally called Wat Pa Kaew, this temple was built in 1357 by King U Thong for monks returning from study in Sri Lanka. Its massive 72-metre chedi was erected by King Naresuan the Great in 1592 to celebrate his single-combat victory over the Burmese crown prince on elephant-back. The grounds feature rows of Buddha statues draped in saffron robes and a large reclining Buddha.
topAttractions.location: Southeast of the island, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya
tips: Still an active temple — monks live and pray here, so be respectful
Climb the large chedi for panoramic views of the surrounding countryside
The rows of saffron-draped Buddha statues make for excellent photos
Located 2 km southeast of the island — easy by bicycle or tuk-tuk

topAttractions.name: Wat Lokayasutharam
topAttractions.description: Home to the largest reclining Buddha in Ayutthaya — a 42-metre-long, 8-metre-high outdoor statue lying in an open field among scattered ruins. Unlike Bangkok's gilded Reclining Buddha at Wat Pho, this one is made of brick and stucco, lending it a raw, weathered grandeur. The surrounding parkland setting with old trees framing the enormous figure makes for dramatic photography.
topAttractions.location: Western part of Ayutthaya Historical Park, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya
tips: Open-air site — no entrance gate or opening hours
Morning light is best for photography (the Buddha faces east)
Local vendors sell lotus flowers and incense for offerings
Combine with nearby Wat Worachettharam on the same cycling route

topAttractions.name: Bang Pa-In Royal Palace
topAttractions.description: Located 20 km south of Ayutthaya, Bang Pa-In is a summer palace complex blending Thai, Chinese, Gothic and Italian Renaissance architectural styles. Originally built by King Prasat Thong in the 17th century, it was abandoned after the fall of Ayutthaya and restored by Kings Mongkut (Rama IV) and Chulalongkorn (Rama V) in the 19th century. The Chinese-style Wehat Chamrun Palace and the Thai pavilion in the middle of the lake are particular highlights.
tips: Open daily 08:00–16:00 (last entry 15:30)
Strict dress code — long trousers/skirts and covered shoulders required; sarongs available for rent
Golf carts available for rent (400 THB) to cover the large grounds
Can be combined with Ayutthaya on a day trip from Bangkok — stop here on the way back

topAttractions.name: Chao Sam Phraya National Museum
topAttractions.description: The main museum in Ayutthaya houses an impressive collection of gold artefacts, Buddha images and art recovered from the city's ruins. Highlights include gold regalia found inside the crypt of Wat Ratchaburana, intricately carved door panels and a collection of bronze Buddha heads. The museum provides essential historical context that brings the temple ruins to life.
topAttractions.location: Rojana Road, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya
tips: Visit before exploring the temples for historical context
Air-conditioned — a good midday refuge from the heat
Open Wednesday–Sunday 09:00–16:00 (closed Monday and Tuesday)
Photography is not allowed inside the main exhibition halls

topAttractions.name: Viharn Phra Mongkhon Bophit
topAttractions.description: This modern viharn (assembly hall) houses one of the largest bronze Buddha images in Thailand — a 17-metre seated figure originally cast in the 15th century. The statue survived the Burmese destruction of 1767, although it was severely damaged by fire. Restored multiple times, it now sits resplendent in gold leaf inside a whitewashed modern building adjacent to Wat Phra Si Sanphet.
topAttractions.location: Adjacent to Wat Phra Si Sanphet, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya
tips: One of the most revered Buddhist sites in Ayutthaya — locals come to pray and make offerings
Remove shoes before entering
Visit immediately after Wat Phra Si Sanphet — they share the same grounds
Open daily 08:00–17:00

travelTips: Buy the 220 THB six-temple combo ticket — it covers Wat Mahathat, Wat Ratchaburana, Wat Phra Si Sanphet, Wat Phra Ram, Wat Maheyong and Wat Chaiwatthanaram, saving money if you visit three or more.
Rent a bicycle for 50–100 THB/day — the island is flat and compact, and cycling is the most enjoyable way to explore at your own pace.
Start your temple tour at 08:00 to beat the heat and day-trip crowds that arrive from Bangkok around 10:00–11:00.
Dress respectfully: cover shoulders and knees at all temples. Carry a lightweight scarf or sarong in your bag.
Bring plenty of water and sunscreen — shade is limited among the ruins, especially at midday.
If short on time, prioritize Wat Mahathat, Wat Phra Si Sanphet and Wat Chaiwatthanaram — together they capture the essence of Ayutthaya.
Evening river cruises (from 200 THB) offer magical views of illuminated temples reflected in the water.
Most temples close at 18:00 — plan sunset at Wat Chaiwatthanaram and time your visit accordingly.
gettingThere: Ayutthaya is just 80 km north of Bangkok, making it one of the easiest day trips in Thailand. Three main options: (1) Train from Bangkok's Hua Lamphong (Krung Thep Aphiwat) station — 3rd class 15–20 THB (1.5–2 hrs, no A/C, very local experience), 2nd class 65 THB, special express 345 THB with A/C; departures roughly every hour. (2) Minivan from Victory Monument — 60–80 THB, 1–1.5 hrs depending on traffic, departs every 30 minutes 06:00–17:00. (3) Organised day tour from Bangkok — 1,200–2,500 THB including transport, guide and lunch. From Suvarnabhumi Airport, take the Airport Rail Link to Phaya Thai (45 THB), then BTS to Mo Chit or taxi to Hua Lamphong for the train. Or book a private transfer directly to Ayutthaya (2,000–2,500 THB, 1.5 hrs).
gettingAround: Bicycle rental: 50–100 THB/day from guesthouses and shops near the train station and on Naresuan Road. The flat terrain and compact island make cycling the ideal way to explore. Tuk-tuk: Negotiate a half-day tour (4 hours, 4–5 temples) for 800–1,200 THB or individual trips for 50–100 THB between nearby temples. Songthaew (shared pickup truck): Local routes around the island for 10–20 THB per person. Long-tail boat: River tours visiting Wat Chaiwatthanaram and riverside temples from 1,000–1,500 THB per boat (seats 6–8 people). Walking: The central island is walkable but distances between temples can be 1–3 km — in the midday heat, cycling or a tuk-tuk is more practical. Motorcycle rental: 200–300 THB/day — useful for reaching Bang Pa-In or temples outside the island, but not necessary for the main circuit.
